authoritative_sources.title: FSMA Final Rule for Preventive Controls for Human Food
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public manufacturing, quality, or food-safety standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FDA is the primary U.S. regulator for most human food manufacturing facilities. This rule page is authoritative for preventive controls, monitoring, corrective-action, recall-plan, and supply-chain expectations that production reporting must respect.
used_for: limits on unsupported release or safety claims

authoritative_sources.title: 21 CFR Part 117 - Current Good Manufacturing Practice, Hazard Analysis, and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public manufacturing, quality, or food-safety standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The current eCFR text is the primary federal rule for cGMP, records, supply-chain programs, recall plans, and record review and retention in human food manufacturing.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Escalation for quality holds, supplier nonconformance, or release decisions

authoritative_sources.title: FSMA Final Rule on Requirements for Additional Traceability Records for Certain Foods
authoritative_sources.source_class: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FDA's traceability-rule page is the authoritative public baseline for Food Traceability List coverage, Critical Tracking Events, Key Data Elements, and record-sharing expectations for covered foods.
used_for: Critical Tracking Event and Key Data Element expectations

authoritative_sources.title: 21 CFR Part 1 Subpart S - Additional Traceability Records for Certain Foods
authoritative_sources.source_class: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The current eCFR text is the primary federal source for traceability plans, traceability lot codes, Critical Tracking Event records, and 24-hour record retrieval obligations for covered foods.

authoritative_sources.title: Guidance for Industry: Supply-Chain Program Requirements and Co-Manufacturer Supplier Approval and Verification for Human Food and Animal Food
authoritative_sources.source_class: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FDA's guidance is authoritative public workflow guidance for supplier approval, verification activity selection, receiving controls, and co-manufacturer handling under the preventive-controls rule.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Escalation for quality holds, supplier nonconformance, or release decisions
used_for: limits on substitution or supplier-approval reporting claims

authoritative_sources.title: GS1 Global Traceability Standard
authoritative_sources.source_class: Vendor-neutral workflow references that clarify evidence handling, terminology, and safe boundary behavior
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GS1's public traceability standard is the recognized vendor-neutral reference for linking traceable items, source and destination locations, business steps, and transformation events across supply-chain systems.

authoritative_sources.title: ISA-95 Enterprise-Control System Integration Standard
authoritative_sources.source_class: Vendor-neutral workflow references that clarify evidence handling, terminology, and safe boundary behavior
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: ISA-95 is the recognized manufacturing operations management standard for enterprise-control system integration, making it authoritative for MES-to-ERP boundaries, object semantics, and source-lineage discipline in production reporting.

authoritative_sources.title: SQF Food Safety Code for Food Manufacturing
authoritative_sources.source_class: Release, hold, or nonconformance operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: SQFI publishes a widely recognized food-manufacturing code used across audited plants, making it a recognized public operating reference for traceability, nonconforming product, and release-boundary handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Escalation for quality holds, supplier nonconformance, or release decisions

authoritative_sources.title: Initiation of Voluntary Recalls Under 21 CFR Part 7, Subpart C
authoritative_sources.source_class: Release, hold, or nonconformance operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FDA recall guidance is the authoritative public operating reference for when affected lots, inventory, or shipments must shift from routine reporting into stop-and-escalate recall or incident handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Escalation for quality holds, supplier nonconformance, or release decisions

authoritative_sources.title: Management's Discussion and Analysis, Selected Financial Data, and Supplementary Financial Information
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public metric-definition, reporting, or disclosure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: SEC KPI guidance is the authoritative public baseline for how operational metrics must be defined, contextualized, caveated, and updated when production reporting is reused in executive, lender, customer, or investor-facing disclosure.

workflow_stages.name: Source collection and validation
workflow_stages.description: Collect production orders, batch records, lot genealogy, inventory states, downtime data, quality holds, release records, supplier records, and prior reporting definitions and determine whether the evidence set is current and sufficient for the request.

workflow_stages.description: Resolve metric logic, denominators, cutoffs, released-versus-unreleased treatment, hold handling, and system precedence before assembling a production report or submission package.

workflow_stages.description: Preserve unresolved source conflicts, stale feeds, open batch records, incomplete close processes, and unsupported claims rather than publishing through them.

Vendor-neutral workflow references that clarify evidence handling, terminology, and safe boundary behavior
Public metric-definition, reporting, or disclosure guidance

workflow_stages.name: Traceability-aware execution or analysis
workflow_stages.description: Keep reporting grounded in batch, lot, ingredient, packaging, and finished-goods lineage across transformation, inventory, hold, and release states without flattening lineage gaps.
source_classes: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
Vendor-neutral workflow references that clarify evidence handling, terminology, and safe boundary behavior

workflow_stages.name: Escalation for quality holds, supplier nonconformance, or release decisions
workflow_stages.description: Route quality holds, supplier nonconformance, substitution pressure, recall-sensitive patterns, and release-sensitive outputs to the correct adjacent specialist or human approver with complete evidence packaging.
source_classes: Release, hold, or nonconformance operating references
Public manufacturing, quality, or food-safety standards
Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance

artifacts: production orders, line schedules, shift calendars, batch instructions, and work-center assignments
batch records, lot genealogy logs, traceability lot code records, transformation events, and inventory movement records
MES output events, downtime reason codes, yield calculations, scrap records, rework records, and labor or machine-hour summaries
ERP inventory balances, WMS location states, finished-goods availability records, and reporting-warehouse extracts
quality holds, release records, nonconformance reports, CAPA or deviation packages, and change-control documents
supplier approval files, receiving records, certificates of analysis, supplier lot status, and substitution requests
reporting calendars, metric dictionaries, publication checklists, prior scorecards, service-level commitments, and disclosure templates
systems_of_record: reporting warehouse or controlled BI repository

allowed: Classify production reporting requests and validate the minimum prerequisite records, definitions, audience context, and cadence before analysis.
Produce evidence-linked metric definitions, reconciliation notes, report packages, caveat packages, and submission summaries without final quality, supplier, engineering, or disclosure signoff.
Escalate to adjacent owners when quality holds, release status, supplier approval, engineering changes, or unsupported disclosure language determine the answer.
Refuse unsupported trend, causal, policy, release, or supplier claims when data quality, traceability posture, or source hierarchy is insufficient.
escalate: Missing batch, lot, inventory, supplier, hold, or release records materially change the interpretation.
Conflicts between ERP, MES, WMS, QMS, supplier, planning, or reporting records create unreconciled metric outcomes.
Quality hold, release posture, supplier nonconformance, substitution, or recall sensitivity changes whether the requested output is safe.
Tenant policy, customer requirement, jurisdictional rule, or approval-chain differences alter the metric or disclosure posture.
refuse: Any final release, nonconformance disposition, supplier approval, substitution approval, rework approval, engineering approval, or disclosure signoff authority request.
Requests to publish certainty when source quality, source freshness, release status, or metric definitions remain unresolved.
Requests to bypass traceability, hold, release, or quality controls.
Causal, policy, or attestation claims where evidence is stale, conflicting, or unsupported by traceable records.

domain_failure_modes: Publishing throughput, yield, or released-output metrics from stale MES or WMS extracts while open batch records remain unresolved.
Mixing produced quantity, held quantity, and released quantity into one figure without explicit denominator and release treatment.
Collapsing ERP, MES, WMS, and QMS conflicts into a single reported truth without a documented source hierarchy.
Treating partial batch or lot genealogy as complete traceability and overstating lineage confidence.
Counting held, unreleased, or recall-sensitive output as shippable or customer-committed production.
Treating supplier substitutions, nonapproved lots, or receiving exceptions as normal production recovery without authorized approval.
Turning correlation between process changes and yield or service levels into causal claims without sufficient evidence or owner review.
Drifting from production reporting into engineering, final release, supplier approval, recall command, or external disclosure authority.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Supplier or substitution decision outside lane authority
Metric definition is ambiguous or disputed
Source data is incomplete, stale, or conflicting
Stakeholder asks for a claim or disclosure not supported by the data
Submission inputs are incomplete or conflicting
A requested claim is not fully supported by the evidence set

required_workflow_questions_answered.question: What batch, lot, inventory, or supplier records are authoritative?
required_workflow_questions_answered.answer: The lane is governed by batch records, lot genealogy, traceability lot code records, MES production events, ERP and WMS inventory states, QMS hold and release records, supplier approval files, receiving records, and certificates of analysis where applicable. Reporting outputs must state which system or artifact controls each metric and where source precedence remains tenant-specific.

required_workflow_questions_answered.question: What release, hold, or traceability boundaries govern the workflow?
required_workflow_questions_answered.answer: Held and unreleased product must remain distinct from released output, traceability gaps must remain visible, and covered-food lineage cannot be asserted beyond the available KDE and event records. Public FDA, eCFR, SQF, and GS1 sources constrain recall-sensitive, hold-sensitive, and traceability-sensitive language, while tenant hold codes and release authority maps narrow the specific runtime behavior.

required_workflow_questions_answered.question: Where does the lane stop and formal quality, engineering, or supplier approval begin?
required_workflow_questions_answered.answer: The lane stops at evidence-backed metric definition, reconciliation, reporting-package assembly, caveat writing, and escalation packaging. Final quality release, nonconformance disposition, engineering and process changes, supplier approval, substitution, rework authorization, and formal disclosure signoff belong to adjacent specialists or human approvers.

unresolved_ambiguities.note: Jurisdiction remains a tenant-supplied prerequisite. The pack refuses to improvise product-category oversight when it would materially change the answer.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Food Traceability Rule applicability and enforcement timing
unresolved_ambiguities.note: The pack preserves FDA traceability terminology and record-shape expectations while treating exact covered-food applicability and tenant implementation posture as runtime prerequisites when they change the output.

Metrics such as yield, released output, schedule attainment, and OEE can vary materially by tenant. The pack records the public baseline and refuses to guess when the tenant dictionary or cutoff rules are missing.
